modal.headline: Glossary
modal.sub: See a word you don't recognize? Look it up here in plain language.
modal.search_placeholder: Search by term (e.g. PRD, traceability, context)
modal.empty: No terms match "{query}". Try a different word.

plan.label: ① Plan
plan.hint: The stage where you sort out what to build from meeting logs

design.label: ② Design
design.hint: The stage where you draw a blueprint of what and how to build

lint.label: ③ Lint
lint.hint: The stage where you check the code was built as designed

deliver.label: ④ Deliverables
deliver.hint: The stage where you bundle the results to hand off to the next team

meeting_log.name: Meeting Log
meeting_log.desc: The starting point of everything. Type out what was discussed in a meeting or upload a recording, and Harness extracts the essentials from it.
meeting_log.example: "This week's decision: card payment only, refunds within 7 days"

cps.name: Core Problem & Solution
cps.desc: A one-page summary distilled from the meeting log to just "what Problem to solve and how (Solution)". Long meetings keep only the decisions that truly matter.
cps.example: Problem: customers don't know how to get a refund → Solution: add a refund button to order history

prd.name: Product Requirements Doc
prd.desc: A requirements document that breaks the CPS down into developable units. Think of it as a list of "what features this product must have".

epic.name: Feature Group
epic.desc: A large chunk grouping related features together. e.g. the "Account management" group contains sign-up, login, and deactivation features.

story.name: User Story
story.desc: A single sentence describing "what the user wants to do". It is the smallest unit of an individual feature.
story.example: "As a customer, I want to request a refund right from my order history"

acceptance_criteria.name: Acceptance Criteria
acceptance_criteria.desc: The checklist that must be satisfied to call a feature "done". Developers and planners judge pass/fail against the same standard.

nfr.name: Non-Functional Requirement
nfr.desc: Quality conditions about "how fast, secure, and resilient" something must be, rather than the feature itself. e.g. "payment within 3 seconds", "password 8+ characters".

rule_generator.name: Coding Rule Generator
rule_generator.desc: AI recommends code-writing rules suited to this project (naming conventions, folder structure, forbidden patterns, etc.). They're later applied automatically during code lint.

spack.name: Spec Pack
spack.desc: The answer to "what to build". It bundles the entry points the system exposes (API), its core data (Entity), and the rules it must keep (Policy).

api.name: External Entry Point
api.desc: The "counter" through which apps, web, or other services request something from this system. It defines what to send to which address and what comes back.

entity.name: Core Data
entity.desc: The business "nouns" the system handles. Things that need to be stored and managed, like users, orders, and payments.

policy.name: Business Rule
policy.desc: A promise the system must always keep. e.g. "retry payment up to 3 times", "no order when stock is 0".

ddd.name: Domain Model
ddd.desc: The answer to "how the system works". It divides the business into areas (Domain-Driven Design) and models each area's data and events.

bounded_context.name: Bounded Context
bounded_context.desc: One chunk of the business. For a shopping mall, "payment", "membership", and "shipping" are each a context, and each has its own vocabulary and rules.

aggregate.name: Aggregate
aggregate.desc: A unit of data handled as one bundle. An "Order" contains order items, payment info, and shipping address — and "Order" is the name representing that whole bundle.

domain_event.name: Domain Event
domain_event.desc: A record that "something just happened". e.g. "payment completed", "order cancelled". Other features watch these events to do follow-up work like notifications or settlement.

architecture.name: System Architecture
architecture.desc: A blueprint drawing the system as actually deployable units. It shows which services and databases exist and how they connect to each other.

vibe_coding.name: Vibe Coding
vibe_coding.desc: Handing your design output wholesale to AI coding tools (Claude Code, Cursor, etc.) to write the code automatically. Harness's design deliverables become that input.

lint.name: Code Lint
lint.desc: An automatic check of whether the code pushed to GitHub follows the design spec. It shows the pass rate across 4 areas — spec, domain, architecture, and coding rules — in a table.

traceability.name: Traceability Score
traceability.desc: The ratio (0–100) of items written in the design (API, data, rules) that actually appear in the code. It measures "was it implemented as designed", not "code quality".

deterministic.name: Deterministic Check
deterministic.desc: A result confirmed with 100% certainty by reading the code directly to see whether something "really exists or not". It's the most trustworthy verdict, not an AI guess.

fix_agent.name: Fix Agent
fix_agent.desc: Collects the rules that failed the lint and turns them into a spec an AI coding tool can fix directly. Paste that spec into the tool and the code fixes proceed automatically.

lineage.name: Code Lineage
lineage.desc: A map tracing how requirements (Story) → spec (API) → domain → actual code files connect together. It shows "why this code was written" at a glance.

coverage.name: Mapping Coverage
coverage.desc: What percentage of design deliverables are linked to actual code files. A low value means there are items "written in the spec but missing in the code".

handoff_zip.name: Handoff Package
handoff_zip.desc: A document bundle packing the design spec + the list of repository addresses + the lint report into one file. The source code itself isn't included; the code is pulled from the repositories.

repository.name: Repository
repository.desc: The GitHub space where code is stored. You can register several by role, such as frontend, backend, DB, and mobile.
